reasoning: ‘Fai moa noa’ parallels examples where ‘kai noa’ translates to ‘have only eaten,’ so here it is rendered as ‘only raised chickens.’

answer: I have also washed what the teacher caught.
reasoning: ‘Kua holoholo foki he faiaoga ne takafaga e au a ia’ indicates washing something the teacher caught, with ‘foki’ meaning ‘also.’

answer: The child will have plenty of canoes that the bird will wash. / The child will also wash the canoes that the bird has.
reasoning: ‘To muhu vaka e tama ka holoholo he moa’ can be interpreted in two ways regarding who has plenty of canoes and who does the washing, so both translations are given.
‘He will also read’ can be expressed with the marker ‘Kua’ (often used for completed/future context), ‘faitau’ (‘to read’), and ‘foki’ (‘also’).

answer: Kua kai noa a Sione e ika ka kai he faiaoga.
reasoning: ‘Sione has only had fish that the teacher will eat’ uses ‘kai noa’ alongside ‘ka kai he faiaoga’ to express that the teacher is the one who will eat it.

answer: Ne tā he faiaoga ne kitia e Mele e vaka.
reasoning: ‘The teacher that Mele saw built the canoe’ is expressed by referencing the teacher Mele saw, who then built the canoe (‘tā he ... e vaka’).
‘There have always been plenty of carpenters’ is formed by ‘muhu tumau’ (‘always plenty’) plus ‘e kámuta’ (‘carpenters’).

answer: Relative clauses follow the noun they modify, tense markers frequently appear before the verb, and ‘muhu’ expresses ‘plenty’ or ‘many.’
